name: Village Path
description: A well-worn dirt path winds toward a village in the distance. A stone well stands nearby, and an old man sits on a rock, staring at you with the unsettling intensity of someone who has been expecting you for decades.

responses.look: A sturdy stone well built from mossy grey stone, its rim worn smooth by generations of hands hauling water. A wooden bucket hangs from a frayed rope, swaying gently over a darkness that seems to go down much further than a well has any right to. You lean over the edge -- the water below is impossibly far down, a tiny circle of reflected sky. Something moves in the depths. Probably your reflection. Probably.
responses.take: The well weighs approximately four tons. Your back weighs approximately one hernia. Let's not.
responses.use: You lower the bucket and haul up some water. It's cold, clear, and does absolutely nothing magical. You notice the bucket came up lighter than expected -- as though something down there took a sip first. How disappointing.
responses.talk: You lean over the well and call down into the darkness. Your voice echoes back, distorted and layered, as though several versions of you are answering from different depths. The last echo sounds nothing like you at all. You step back from the edge.
responses.open: The well is open-topped. Very open. Dangerously open, one might say. There's no grate, no cover, no safety rail. Just a round hole into darkness, trusting that everyone who approaches it will exercise good judgment. In this kingdom, that's optimistic.
responses.push: You shove the well. Several centuries of stonework remain unmoved. Your dignity, however, is rattled.
responses.pull: You pull at the well rope. It rattles and clinks, descending into darkness. From far below comes a distant splash, then silence. Then -- was that a second splash? The well gives nothing back. It never does.
responses.default: You regard the well thoughtfully. The well regards you back with silent, stony indifference. The darkness inside it is the patient kind.

responses.look: A weathered signpost at the path's fork. One arrow points south toward 'Cave' and another north toward 'Village.' A third arrow, pointing straight down, reads 'You Are Here.' Helpful.
responses.take: You try to uproot the signpost. It's cemented in. The local council may be incompetent at governance, but their signpost installation is impeccable.
responses.use: It's a signpost. You read it. That's... that's how you use a signpost.
responses.default: You study the signpost as though it contains the secrets of the universe. It does not. It contains directions.
responses.talk: You read the signpost aloud. 'Village to the north. Forest to the south. Certain death: optional.' The signpost doesn't respond. It's done its job. Whether you listen is your problem.
responses.open: It's a wooden post with a plank nailed to it. There's nothing to open. Unless you mean the possibility of going in the direction it points, in which case -- it's already open.
responses.push: You shove the signpost. It leans drunkenly to one side, now pointing 'Village' toward the ground and 'Forest' toward the sky. You've improved nothing.
responses.pull: You yank the signpost. It resists, rooted in the ground by years of compacted dirt. The signpost has more commitment to this spot than you have to anything.

responses.look: The forest beyond the path is thick and unwelcoming. The trees crowd together like gossips, and the undergrowth is the kind that swallows ankles and ambitions alike. A faded sign reads: 'CERTAIN DEATH THIS WAY.'
responses.use: You gaze into the dark forest and wisely decide against entering. Your survival instincts, dormant for most of your life, have chosen this moment to awaken.
responses.default: You peer into the dark forest. Something peers back. You both pretend this didn't happen.
responses.take: You grab at the forest edge. Branches scratch your hands. Leaves crumble. You can't take a forest. You can barely take a hint.
responses.talk: You call into the forest. Something rustles in response. Then silence. The forest is not interested in conversation. It has its own dark business.
responses.open: You can't open a forest. Forests are already open, in the sense that they're outdoors. The trees don't have a door policy.
responses.push: You push at the undergrowth. Branches bend and snap back. The forest gives ground reluctantly and reclaims it immediately. It's been practising this for centuries.
responses.pull: You pull at branches and brambles. They tangle in your fingers and clothes. The forest edge doesn't want to come with you, and it's making that abundantly clear.

responses.look: Green hills roll toward the horizon with the gentle, pastoral beauty of a kingdom that hasn't noticed it's falling apart. The grass sways in the breeze, deceptively peaceful, as though nothing could possibly go wrong in a place this pretty. History disagrees.
responses.take: You want to take the hills. Perhaps fold them up and put them in your pocket. This is not how geography works.
responses.use: You could roll down them, but you're an adult on a quest, not a child at a picnic. Though the distinction blurs more with each passing hour.
responses.default: You survey the rolling hills. They're green, they're pleasant, and they're hiding something. Landscapes this pretty always are.
responses.talk: You shout across the hills. The wind carries your voice away into grass and distance. The hills don't answer. They never do. It's part of their charm.
responses.open: The rolling hills stretch in every direction, already as open as landscape gets. There's no door in a hill. There's no mechanism to reveal. It's just... earth.
responses.push: You can't push the hills. They're terrain. Large terrain. The kind measured in acres, not effort.
responses.pull: You can't pull the hills closer. They maintain their distance with the quiet stubbornness of geography.

responses.look: A well-worn dirt path winds into the distance, packed hard by countless feet that had somewhere more important to be. Ruts from cart wheels suggest commerce. Boot prints suggest purpose. Your footprints suggest aimless wandering.
responses.take: You scoop up a handful of dirt from the path. Congratulations. You now own dirt. Your portfolio is really coming together.
responses.use: You follow the path with your eyes as it disappears around a bend. It's been leading people to the village for generations, asking nothing in return. The path has a better work ethic than most people you've met.
responses.default: You examine the dirt path. It's dirt. It's a path. Together, they exceed the sum of their parts, but only just.
responses.talk: You talk to the path. It stretches on in patient silence. Paths don't talk. They just lead -- sometimes to good places, sometimes to certain death. This one seems undecided.
responses.open: Paths don't open. They're already open. That's their defining feature. A closed path is just 'ground.'
responses.push: You push at the dirt. It's dirt. On the ground. Where dirt lives. This is the natural order of things.
responses.pull: You scrabble at the path. Dirt comes up under your nails. You have not improved the path. You have, however, made a small hole.

responses.look: Deep ruts in the dirt, left by cart wheels that passed this way when the path still saw regular traffic. The tracks are old enough to have developed their own ecosystem of weeds. They lead north toward the village and south into uncertainty.
responses.take: You can't take cart tracks. They're impressions in dirt. This is not the kind of treasure you were hoping for.
responses.use: You follow the cart tracks with your eyes. They don't lead anywhere you aren't already going.
responses.talk: You address the cart tracks. They lie there silently, being tracks. If they could speak, they'd probably complain about the traffic. Or the lack thereof.
responses.open: Cart tracks don't open. They're grooves in the ground. Your expectations of the ground continue to be unrealistic.
responses.push: You stomp on the cart tracks. The dirt compresses slightly. You've altered history's record by approximately nothing.
responses.pull: You can't pull tracks out of the ground. They're not tracks in the removable sense. They're geological autobiography.

responses.look: Scraggly wildflowers line the path, doing their best to be cheerful in a kingdom where the ground itself is slowly turning to stone. Some are genuinely colourful. Others have given up and gone grey at the edges, like optimism in its final stage.
responses.take: You pick a wildflower. It wilts immediately in your hand, as though freedom was the one thing it couldn't survive. You feel slightly guilty.
responses.use: You sniff a wildflower. It smells faintly of earth and resignation. Not unpleasant, but not exactly a perfume you'd bottle.
responses.talk: You compliment the wildflowers on their persistence. They sway in the breeze, which you choose to interpret as modest acknowledgment.
responses.open: Flowers don't open on command. They open on their own schedule, which is more than can be said for most doors in this kingdom.
responses.push: You push through the wildflowers. They bend and spring back, unbothered. Plants have a resilience you frankly envy.
responses.pull: You uproot a wildflower. The earth releases it with a small sigh. Now you have a dying flower and a tiny hole. Achievement unlocked.

responses.look: A wooden bucket dangles from a frayed rope over the well. It's seen better days -- the wood is swollen, one iron band has rusted through, and it lists to one side like a sailor after shore leave. The inside is stained dark from years of hauling water from whatever's down there. Still, it holds together through sheer stubborn refusal to be useless.
responses.take: The bucket is tied to the well rope with a knot that's half seamanship, half desperation. You'd need to cut it free, and cutting the only bucket seems short-sighted even by your standards. Besides, something tells you the bucket is happier up here than down there.
responses.use: You lower the bucket into the well. It descends into darkness, the rope creaking as it unspools. Silence. Then a hollow splash, further down than you expected. When you haul it back up, the bucket is half-full of dark, cold water. The bucket leaks slightly, as if weeping for its former glory. Or warning you.
responses.talk: You address the bucket. It swings on its rope, creaking. This is either acknowledgment or wind. You choose to believe the bucket heard you. It's been down there and back more times than you'd care to count. If buckets could talk, this one would have stories.
responses.open: It's a bucket. It's already open. That's the defining feature of buckets -- the top is off. Permanently. By design.
responses.push: You push the bucket. It swings out over the well, pendulum-like, hanging above the darkness below. For a moment it seems to pause there, as though considering the drop. Then it swings back. The bucket is patient. The bucket always returns.
responses.pull: You pull the bucket toward you. The rope goes taut, the bucket resists, and when you let go it swings back over the well with a reproachful creak. It knows where it belongs.

responses.look: A frayed rope connects the bucket to a weathered crossbeam above the well. Several strands have already given up, leaving the rope looking like it's one strong tug away from a career change. The remaining fibres cling together with the determination of things that know they're all that stands between a bucket and oblivion. You wouldn't trust it with a bucket, let alone a person.
responses.take: The rope is the only thing holding the bucket over the well. Taking it would strand the bucket, and the bucket doesn't deserve that. Besides, the rope looks like it would snap if asked to support anything heavier than regret.
responses.use: You pull the rope to lower the bucket. It creaks, strands protest, but it holds. Barely. The rope has survived this long by supporting nothing heavier than a wooden bucket. It'd appreciate you not testing its limits further.
responses.talk: You address the rope. It sways slightly. Ropes are not known for their conversational skills, but this one has a certain frayed dignity. It's held on longer than most relationships in this kingdom.
responses.open: You can't open a rope. You can untie it, cut it, or fray it further, but opening is not in a rope's repertoire.
responses.push: You push the rope. It sways over the dark mouth of the well. This is the full extent of rope-pushing outcomes. You've mastered it.
responses.pull: You pull the rope. The bucket descends into the well. The rope groans. Somewhere, a strand snaps. The rope forgives you, but it's keeping count. One day the count will reach zero.

responses.look: A faded wooden sign nailed to a tree at the forest edge reads: 'CERTAIN DEATH BEYOND THIS POINT.' Someone has crossed out 'CERTAIN' and written 'PROBABLE' in smaller letters. Someone else has crossed out 'PROBABLE' and written 'ENTERTAINING.' The optimism is almost admirable.
responses.take: You pry at the sign. The nails hold. Whoever posted this warning about certain death was very certain about the sign staying put.
responses.use: You read the sign again. 'CERTAIN DEATH.' 'PROBABLE DEATH.' 'ENTERTAINING DEATH.' Each amendment tells a story. None of the stories end well.
responses.talk: You read the sign aloud. 'Certain death beyond this point.' The forest rustles. Was that agreement? Anticipation? Wind? Best not to ask.
responses.open: It's a sign, not a book. Its entire message is on the front. There's no sequel, no appendix, no hopeful postscript.
responses.push: You push the sign flat against the tree. The word 'DEATH' faces the bark. Out of sight, but not out of forest.
responses.pull: You pull the sign. The nails screech but hold. The sign has been warning travellers for years and isn't about to stop for your curiosity.

actions.text: You lower the bucket into the well. The rope creaks and unspools into darkness. A splash, far below. You wait. When you haul the bucket back up, it's heavier than expected. Inside, flopping weakly in an inch of dark water, is a pale, translucent fish. It's completely blind -- two smooth patches of skin where eyes should be -- and it wriggles with the confused energy of something that has never encountered 'above' before. It's not pretty, but it's alive. And if there's a hungry creature somewhere nearby, this might be exactly what it wants.

You lower the bucket into the well. The rope creaks and unspools into darkness. A splash, far below. You wait. When you haul the bucket back up, something is flapping inside -- a pale, eyeless fish, translucent as old glass. It thrashes weakly in an inch of cold water, bewildered by the sudden change in altitude. You pocket the fish carefully. Something tells you a creature with an empty stomach would find this irresistible.

You pull the rope, lowering the bucket into the well's darkness. After a long wait and a distant splash, you haul it back up. Inside: a pale, blind cave fish, flopping in cold water. Its skin is almost transparent -- you can see its tiny heart beating with confused urgency. You wrap it carefully. Whatever's hungry enough to block a road might find this persuasive.

lost-death.title: Death by Poor Navigation
lost-death.narratorText: You wander off the path into the deep forest. The sign that said 'CERTAIN DEATH THIS WAY' was, in retrospect, being entirely literal. The trees close behind you like a curtain. The last sound you hear is a bird, singing what you can only assume is a eulogy. It's not a very good one.

well-death.title: Death by Excessive Curiosity
well-death.narratorText:
You swing a leg over the well's rim and grab the rope, which immediately announces its resignation with a decisive snap. For a brief, beautiful moment you are suspended in the air, held aloft by nothing but optimism. Then gravity remembers you exist.

The fall is long enough to reflect on your choices. The well is much deeper than it looked -- impossibly deep, really, as though the earth itself opened up to swallow you. The circle of sky above shrinks to a coin, then a pin-prick, then nothing.

The splash, when it comes, is surprisingly dignified. The water is freezing. The walls are smooth. There is no way back up.

Somewhere above, the old man shakes his head. 'They always go for the well,' he mutters. The bucket, now ropeless, sits on the rim -- the only survivor of your expedition.

dynamicDescriptions.met_old_man: A well-worn dirt path winds between the cave and the village. The old man sits on his rock, watching you with an expression that suggests he knows exactly how many times you've died. Which is unsettling, because it's probably accurate.

tiers: That stone toad in the village square looks hungry. Very hungry. Perhaps it would move if it had something to eat. Something from the well, maybe -- there are things living in the dark water down there.
Lower the bucket into the well. There are cave fish living in the deep water. Catch one and the toad in the village might find it appetizing enough to move.
Use the well or the bucket to lower it into the water. You'll pull up a pale cave fish. Then take it to the village square and give it to the stone toad blocking the east road.

responses.look: An old man sits on a rock by the path, watching you with the kind of intensity normally reserved for chess grandmasters and cats who've spotted a bird. His clothes are patched so many times they're more patch than cloth.
responses.talk: The old man peers at you. 'Heading to the village? Watch yourself. The square's fountain turned folk to stone last week. The castle's worse.' He pauses. 'But you look like the sort who ignores good advice. Go north. See for yourself.'
responses.use: You're not sure how one 'uses' an old man. He's not a tool. He barely qualifies as furniture.
responses.take: The old man gives you a look that could curdle milk. He is not going anywhere, and neither are your chances of dignified social interaction.
responses.default: The old man returns to staring at the horizon with the patience of someone who's been doing it professionally for decades.

npcs.defaultResponse: The old man returns to staring at the horizon.
